title: Say Hello to the Jungle
instructions: Use the print() function to output exactly the string "Hello, world" to the console.
whyThisMatters: The print() statement is the fundamental way a programmer inspects values and communicates with user dashboards. Every major software journey starts with standard console output.
errorTips: Make sure you type exactly print("Hello, world"). Double check quotes and capitalization!

title: Printing Numbers & Math
instructions: Output the mathematical product of multiplying 5 by 10 directly inside a print statement. e.g. print(5 * 10).
whyThisMatters: Python evaluates math expressions inside arguments before executing functions. This inline computation is extremely fast and saves storing redundant variables.
errorTips: Provide the multiplication formula inside the print parenthesis, e.g. 5 * 10. Do not write quotes, otherwise it will print the formula text instead of the math result!

title: Storing Jungle Fruits
instructions: Create a variable named fruits and set its value to 25. On the next line, print the variable's value: print(fruits).
whyThisMatters: Variables store data in temporary memory blocks so they can be easily manipulated or referenced repeatedly in long scripts.
errorTips: Ensure you assign it exactly: fruits = 25, and then run print(fruits) without wrapping the variable name in quotes.

title: Simple Fruit Adder
instructions: Add two variables: apples = 10 and oranges = 15. Store the sum in a variable named total, then print total.
whyThisMatters: Mathematical addition across memory cells is the mechanical basis of web checkouts, inventory updates, and database calculations.
template:
apples = 10
oranges = 15
# Compute total and print it below
errorTips: Write total = apples + oranges and then print(total). Double check variable spelling!

title: String Length Check
instructions: Count the number of characters in the string "Supercalifragilistic" using the built-in len() function, and output the length using a print statement.
whyThisMatters: Checking length enables validation parameters, string truncation safeguards, and size indexing boundaries on servers.
errorTips: Pass the variable or literal string into len() inside your print, like: print(len(word)).

title: Jungle Pack Lists
instructions: Initialize a Python list named pack containing standard string items "rope", "map", and "canteen". Print the list to inspect it.
whyThisMatters: Arrays and list collections group multiple related elements into a single variable index, laying the track for dynamic loop runs.
template: # Create list and print it below
errorTips: Declare list with square brackets: pack = ["rope", "map", "canteen"] and then print(pack).

D) Never — for loops are always better

quizQuestions.question: undefined
quizQuestions.sampleAnswer: Windows uses backslashes (\) in paths while Linux/Mac use forward slashes (/). Path('dir') / 'file.txt' automatically uses the correct separator for the OS it runs on. String concatenation with '/' can break on Windows.
